summary: Hiroshima Prefecture is where I was born in 1897. I grew up there in a farming family that had once been large but became quite poor by the time I was growing up. It was a simple rural life, and my family belonged to the Nishi Honganji Buddhist temple there. Despite hardships, it was my starting point before I moved to Tokyo and eventually America.

I came to America in 1918, the year World War I ended. The war was between Germany and Japan, and it influenced my immigration experience. Because of the war, America was short of labor, which helped me stay by saying I would work. The war's end marked a turning point in my life as I arrived in the U.S. during that time.

After landing in America in August 1918, I went to Suisun City to work as an apple cutter and also gathered prunes. It was the season of fruits, and I did manual labor to support myself. This was my first work experience in America, and it was tough but necessary for my survival.

I spent some time in Tokyo before coming to America, aiming to attend college there. However, my family’s bankruptcy forced me to stop school and support myself. I worked as a houseboy in Tokyo but struggled with loneliness and my dialect being misunderstood. I also suffered from beriberi while there, which made me return home temporarily before finally leaving for America.

I had an uncle living in Hawaii who helped finance my studies in America. When I arrived in Honolulu, I expected to meet him, but he was on another island and unavailable. This caused me some trouble and financial strain, as I had to stay in a hotel and send telegrams trying to contact him. Hawaii was a significant link between my family in Japan and my new life in America.

My family belonged to the Nishi Honganji, the West Central Buddhism Temple, back in Hiroshima Prefecture. We were Buddhists by tradition, but I personally converted to Christianity in the 9th grade. So while Nishi Honganji was my family’s religious home, my own faith journey took a different path.

I became a Christian during my 9th grade after attending a Methodist church near my aunt's house. My English was weak, but with tutoring from Mr. Shimose and encouragement from the minister Seki Matsushita, I was baptized before leaving for America. Christianity became an important part of my life, giving me strength and community during difficult times.

The Methodist Church near my aunt’s dry-goods store played a big role in my conversion to Christianity. Mr. Shimose, who taught English at my junior high, lived there and tutored me. The minister, Seki Matsushita, encouraged me to be baptized before I left for America, which I did. The church was a place of learning and spiritual support during my youth.

Seki Matsushita was the minister at the Methodist church I attended. He had a long white beard and was very kind to me. Before I left for America, he advised me to get baptized, assuring me that even if I lost faith, I would return to Jesus Christ. His words stayed with me and gave me courage during my transition to America.

Tasuku Harada was the President of Doshisha University and helped me greatly by introducing me to people in America. He knew many bankers and others who supported me after I arrived. His connections eased my transition and gave me hope for my studies and work in the U.S.

Doshisha University was where Tasuku Harada was president, and through him, I received introductions that helped me in America. Although I did not attend Doshisha myself, the university indirectly supported my journey by connecting me with helpful people. It was part of the network that made my immigration smoother.

On the boat to America, I met Dr. Matsumoto, a professor from Tokyo University who was traveling in first class. He was kind enough to help me when I was seasick and unfamiliar with European food. His kindness was a bright spot during my difficult voyage to America.

When I arrived in San Francisco, I was sent to the Immigration Office on Treasure Island because I traveled third class. We were examined thoroughly there for over a week, surrounded by wire fences so we couldn't escape. It was a stressful time, but I passed all the examinations and was finally allowed to enter America.

I arrived in Honolulu expecting to meet my uncle who was supposed to help me get to Chicago, but he was on another island. This left me stranded and short on money, forcing me to stay in a hotel and send telegrams to find him. It was a difficult and lonely experience before I continued on to the mainland.

Before leaving Japan, I took my parents sightseeing around the Kansai area, including Osaka, Kyoto, and Nara. I wanted to spend time with them, feeling that I might never see them again. We visited many places despite the rain, and my father finally told me to take a break because I was spending too much money.

Kyoto was one of the places I visited with my parents before leaving Japan. We toured many sites there as part of our farewell trip. It was a meaningful time for me to share with my family before my long journey to America.

Nara was another city I took my parents to visit before I left Japan for America. It was part of our sightseeing tour in the Kansai region. These visits were important to me because I felt I might not see them again for a long time.

Although Shakespeare was not directly mentioned in my story, I was interested in English and studied it in school. My English was weak at first, which made me struggle, but I worked hard with help from tutors. Learning English was important for my adaptation to America.

The Shunyoh Maru was the ship I traveled on to America in 1918. The third-class cabin was dark and shaky, which made me seasick even though I was used to rowing boats. Despite the rough conditions, I managed to endure the voyage and arrived safely in San Francisco.

I had permission to enter the Amoa Institute in Chicago, a technology school founded by Mr. Amoa. My uncle in Hawaii was supposed to help me get there after arriving in America. Chicago was my intended destination for further education and study.

The Amoa Institute in Chicago was the technology school I planned to attend after coming to America. It was founded by Mr. Amoa, and I had already received permission to enter before my departure. This school represented my hope for education and a better future in the U.S.
